name: The Euclidean Algorithm — Fast GCD
whatIsIt: The EUCLIDEAN ALGORITHM finds the GCD (greatest common divisor, the HCF) of two numbers super fast, without listing factors. The rule: gcd(a, b) = gcd(b, a mod b). Keep replacing the pair by (the smaller number, the remainder) until the remainder is 0 — the last non-zero remainder is the GCD. A bonus fact (Bézout) says the gcd can always be written as ax + by for some integers x, y.
whyNeeded: For big numbers, listing factors is hopeless — Euclid's method gets the GCD in a few steps. It underpins fractions in lowest terms, Diophantine equations, and modular inverses, and is a staple of Olympiad number theory.
realLifeUses: Reducing a big fraction to lowest terms quickly
Tiling a rectangle with the largest possible equal squares
Sharing two amounts into the largest equal groups
Gears and timing problems that repeat together

vocabulary.meaning: The greatest common divisor — the largest number dividing both.
Two numbers with GCD 1 (no common factor except 1).
gcd(a, b) = ax + by for some integers x and y.

story.title: The Largest Tile
story.text: Sohan must tile a 48 by 18 cm strip with the largest equal SQUARE tiles. 'List all factors?' he groans. Miss shows him Euclid: 'gcd(48, 18): 48 mod 18 = 12. Now gcd(18, 12): 18 mod 12 = 6. Then gcd(12, 6): 12 mod 6 = 0 — stop! The last non-zero remainder, 6, is the GCD.' Sohan grins: '6 cm tiles — found in three steps, no factor lists.'
story.extractedProblem: Use the Euclidean algorithm to find gcd(48, 18).
story.answerInStory: 6 — because 48 mod 18 = 12, 18 mod 12 = 6, 12 mod 6 = 0, so the last non-zero remainder is 6.

grids.label: 48×18 strip fills exactly with 6×6 squares → gcd = 6

visual.caption: The GCD is the side of the largest square that tiles an a×b rectangle exactly. Euclid finds it fast by chasing remainders.

teachingGallery.title: Chase the remainders
teachingGallery.note: Replace (a, b) by (b, a mod b) until 0.

standardMethod.summary: Repeatedly replace the larger number by the remainder: gcd(a, b) = gcd(b, a mod b). Stop when the remainder is 0; the last non-zero remainder is the GCD.
steps: Divide the larger by the smaller and note the remainder.
Replace the pair with (smaller number, remainder).
The last non-zero remainder is the GCD.

workedExamples.problem: Find gcd(48, 18) by the Euclidean algorithm.

commonMistakes.fix: GCD is the LARGEST common factor; LCM is the SMALLEST common multiple. Euclid's algorithm finds the GCD.
Keep going until the remainder is 0. The GCD is the LAST NON-ZERO remainder.

easy.q: Euclid replaces gcd(a, b) with gcd(b, …).

challenge.q: If gcd(a, b) = 1, the numbers are…

questions.q: GCD 1 means the two numbers are…

teachBackPrompt: Pick two numbers like 48 and 18 and show a grown-up Euclid's chase: 48 mod 18 = 12, 18 mod 12 = 6, 12 mod 6 = 0, so the GCD is 6. Explain why the last non-zero remainder is the answer.

revisionCard.summary: Euclidean algorithm: gcd(a, b) = gcd(b, a mod b); repeat until remainder 0; the last non-zero remainder is the GCD. GCD 1 ⇒ coprime. Bézout: gcd = ax + by. It is the fast way to reduce fractions and solve number-theory problems.

rememberIt.hook: gcd(a,b) = gcd(b, a mod b) → chase remainders to 0
rememberIt.unpack: Last non-zero remainder is the GCD. GCD 1 = coprime.

studentQuestions.q: Why is it faster than listing factors?
studentQuestions.a: Each step shrinks the numbers a lot, so even huge pairs reach the GCD in just a handful of divisions.
It says gcd(a,b) = ax + by, which lets you solve equations like 'find x, y with ax + by = gcd' — key for modular inverses.

formulas.name: Euclidean step
formulas.formula: gcd(a, b) = gcd(b, a mod b)
